parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Allyn, dau. of Capt. Thomas Allyn of Windsor and Abigail (dau. of Rev. John Warham).
parents.father_research_notes: Ebenezer Gilbert, merchant of Hartford (d.1726). Will inventoried £4,455 including 4 enslaved persons; served on 1717-1718 school committee. Not added as separate entry.

narrative_blocks.header: He lived to 96
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert was baptized in Hartford in 1707 and died in Berlin, Connecticut in 1803 at approximately 96. His son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. His grandson Sylvanus, son of Ebenezer, enlisted in the same army and also died in service.

narrative_blocks.header: His father's 1726 will inventoried four enslaved people
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert's father Ebenezer Gilbert was a Hartford merchant who died in 1726. His estate of £4,455 included four enslaved people itemized by price: a negro (£100), a negro woman (£45), a boy (£100), a child (£30). He had also served on a 1717-1718 committee that divided Kensington into five school districts — using Gilbert's River as a boundary line.

cross_connections.display_label: His daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert was baptized in Hartford in 1707 and died in Berlin, Connecticut in 1803 at approximately 96. His son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. His grandson Sylvanus, son of Ebenezer, enlisted in the same army and also died in service.

narrative_blocks.body: Moses Gilbert's father Ebenezer Gilbert was a Hartford merchant who died in 1726. His estate of £4,455 included four enslaved people itemized by price: a negro (£100), a negro woman (£45), a boy (£100), a child (£30). He had also served on a 1717-1718 committee that divided Kensington into five school districts — using Gilbert's River as a boundary line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband Moses lived to 96
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hooker married Moses Gilbert in February 1734 and bore him six children in Berlin, Connecticut. Her son Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776, at age 34. Ebenezer's own son Sylvanus later died in the same army.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece by brother Samuel Jr. who married her son Hooker.
Her daughter Sarah married my son Hooker Gilbert in 1807.

parents.father_research_notes: Deacon Isaac North of Kensington CT. Per Edward Hooker 1909.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Woodford North of Kensington CT. Per Edward Hooker 1909.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at age ten in Berlin 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Lydia died on January 11, 1776 in Berlin Connecticut at age ten years and four months. Her three siblings — Mary, Chloe, and a third — survived her, but Thomas Gilbert and Mary North raised the surviving children through the Revolutionary War years.

cross_connections.display_label: Elizabeth Hubbard Leete: her mother was a Hooker descendant (Esther.
Elizabeth Hubbard Leete: her mother was a Hooker descendant (Esther.

narrative_blocks.header: Eleven children including replacement Roxana naming
narrative_blocks.body: She bore eleven documented children with Miles Leete between 1792 and 1811. After daughter Roxana died at three, the family used the name Roxana again for a second daughter born 1806 — a 'replacement naming' pattern common to eighteenth-century New England families.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at Peru New York after Connecticut marriage
narrative_blocks.body: She married Miles Leete at Guilford Connecticut about 1791, but died at Peru in upstate New York on 20 February 1827 at age fifty-seven. The migration from coastal Connecticut to the Adirondack-frontier town of Peru tracked a common New England migration route via the Champlain Valley.

narrative_blocks.header: Youngest daughter Elizabeth lived to ninety-four
narrative_blocks.body: Her last child Elizabeth Leete was born in 1811 and lived to ninety-four in 1905 — a span of nearly a century from the early Republic to the dawn of the twentieth century. The mother died less than two decades after Elizabeth's birth, in 1827.

military_service.notes: Per Edward Hooker 1909: 'in the Revolutionary Army, probably in Continental service as no record of his service or death is found in Connecticut.' Date and place of death undocumented.

narrative_blocks.header: Continental Army with no Connecticut record
narrative_blocks.body: Ozias served in the Revolutionary Army — Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy notes no service record was found in Connecticut, suggesting he enlisted in Continental rather than state forces. His date and place of death are also undocumented, leaving a gap in the family record.

parents.grandfather_notes: William Leete — Governor of Connecticut Colony 1676-1683

bio.bio_blurb: Governor Leete’s descendant who married a Hooker line

narrative_blocks.header: His family and the Hookers shared a governor
narrative_blocks.body: He descended from Governor William Leete of Connecticut, whose daughter Mercy had married into the Hooker family generations before — so when he married a Hooker descendant, two of the colony’s founding lines met again in one household.

narrative_blocks.header: He and his son both died
narrative_blocks.body: Ebenezer Gilbert was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. He was 34. His son Sylvanus, then just 13, grew up to serve in the same army — and also died in service.

narrative_blocks.header: His family built a red brick house around
narrative_blocks.body: The Gilbert family built a red brick house in Berlin, Connecticut, probably around 1709. The bricks were made on the farm itself, and Gilbird's River now washes around into the clay pit. Unusually hard and black, some bricks are used as ornament and 'shine today as if enamelled' — the process by which they were made is now lost.

narrative_blocks.header: She married a second Revolutionary soldier after
narrative_blocks.body: When Ebenezer Gilbert was killed in the Revolutionary Army in February 1776, Mary Butrick was 32, with two young children. On November 19, 1778, she married Lieutenant Elisha Booth, another Revolutionary War soldier, and moved with him to Hartland, Connecticut. After Lieutenant Booth's death, she returned alone in 1800 to the old Gilbert place in Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: His father was killed in the Revolutionary War
narrative_blocks.body: Sylvanus Gilbert was 13 years old when his father Ebenezer was killed in the Revolutionary Army on February 15, 1776. He grew up and enlisted himself, serving in the same war. He also died in service — the History of Berlin records: 'Their son Sylvanus, born February 10, 1763, also died in the army.' Father and son both fell in the Revolution.

death.notes: Died unmarried per EH; ~60yr lifespan estimated; born among siblings 1736-1754

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Maternal uncle whose daughter Sarah he married in 1807.

naming_inspiration.description: Given the surname 'Hooker' as his first name in honor of his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert's family. First-name use of the maternal surname is unusual and emphatic — he later married into the Hooker line as well, his second wife Sarah Hooker (H00432) being his maternal first cousin.

military_service.unit: Multiple — Capt. Gad Stanley's Co (1776), Capt. Lankton's Co under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regt (1777), Capt. Matthew Cole's Co (1779)
military_service.notes: Three documented enlistments: (1) June 1776 — four months in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; (2) summer 1777 — three months in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; (3) July 1779 — ten days in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven (Tryon's Raid). Placed on the federal pension list in 1838. DAR Ancestor #A045110. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Revolutionary War enlistments and 1838 pension
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert enlisted three times during the Revolution: four months in June 1776 in Capt. Gad Stanley's Company at the Battle of Harlem Heights; three months in summer 1777 in Capt. Lankton's Company under Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment; and ten days in July 1779 in Capt. Matthew Cole's Company at the Battle of New Haven during Tryon's Raid. He was placed on the federal pension list in 1838 and is recorded as DAR Ancestor #A045110.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin Sarah Hooker in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert's first wife Candace Sage died May 15, 1805. Two years later on June 30, 1807 he married Sarah Hooker — his maternal first cousin and the daughter of his uncle Samuel Hooker Jr. The marriage united two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker via his daughter Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert and his son Samuel Hooker Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Both spouses died within two days December 1840
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker died December 4, 1840 at Kensington Connecticut at age 77. Hooker Gilbert followed her two days later on December 6, 1840 at age 86 — together they had been married for thirty-three years.

narrative_blocks.header: Two grandchildren of Samuel Hooker reunited 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker Gilbert and Sarah Hooker were the marriage point where two branches of the Hooker line came back together. His mother Elizabeth and her father Samuel Jr. had been siblings — both children of Samuel Hooker of Kensington. The cousin marriage produced no documented children of its own but closed a fifty-year gap between the two branches.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

cross_connections.display_label: Aunt and mother-in-law via her son's 1807 cousin marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin in late life
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker married Hooker Gilbert on June 30, 1807 in Kensington when she was 44 — her only documented marriage. Hooker Gilbert was her maternal first cousin: her father Samuel Hooker Jr. and his mother Elizabeth Hooker Gilbert were siblings. The couple lived together for thirty-three years without documented children; Sarah died December 4, 1840, and Hooker Gilbert followed two days later.

narrative_blocks.header: Died weeks after father's remarriage in 1807
narrative_blocks.body: Bathsheba died on July 26, 1807 in Kensington at age twenty-two — less than a month after her father Hooker Gilbert married his second wife Sarah Hooker on June 30, 1807. Her own mother Candace Sage had died two years earlier in 1805, so the young woman's last weeks coincided with the family's transition from old wife to new.

naming_inspiration.description: Carries the 'Hooker' middle name continuing his father Hooker Gilbert's emphatic naming honor. Both father and son carry the Hooker surname as a given/middle name.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Christian Lane Cemetery

death.cause: Likely mercury poisoning from husband's tinsmithing trade

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Candace Sage Gilbert, who died in 1805.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-three from her husband's trade
narrative_blocks.body: Candace died at age forty-three in 1835 — one year after her husband Solomon Churchill Jr., a tinsmith. Family record attributes both deaths to the mercury used in tin manufacturing. Together they had traveled town to town selling their wares, with their five children left orphaned in 1835.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother corrected per Find-a-Grave memorial 21544682: born September 27, 1787 (not 1812 as earlier FS data suggested) — making him an M1 child of Candace Sage, NOT an M2 child of Sarah Hooker. EH 1909's spelling 'David Stage Gilbert' is a transcription error for 'David Sage Gilbert' — the middle name 'Sage' carries his mother's maiden surname forward, matching the same naming pattern as his sister Chloe Sage Gilbert.
